js.variables.response:
JavaScript has three ways to declare variables:

• var — function-scoped, hoisted, can be re-declared. Avoid in modern JS.
• let — block-scoped, not hoisted to a usable state, cannot be re-declared in the same scope.
• const — block-scoped, must be assigned at declaration, the binding cannot be reassigned (but objects/arrays it points to are still mutable).

Rule of thumb: always use const by default; switch to let only when you need to reassign; never use var.
js.variables.admin_notes: Most common beginner question. Emphasise const-first mindset.
examples: what is the difference between var let and const
when should i use let vs const
explain variable declarations in javascript
what does block scoped mean
why should i avoid var in modern javascript
can i reassign a const variable
difference between let and var hoisting

js.data_types.response:
JavaScript has 8 data types, split into two groups:

Primitive (immutable, stored by value):
• string — text: 'hello'
• number — integers and floats: 42, 3.14
• bigint — very large integers: 9007199254740991n
• boolean — true or false
• undefined — variable declared but not assigned
• null — intentional absence of a value
• symbol — unique, immutable identifier

Reference (stored by reference):
• object — includes arrays, functions, dates, maps, etc.

Use typeof to check a value's type at runtime.
js.data_types.code_example:
console.log(typeof 'hello');        // 'string'
console.log(typeof 42);             // 'number'
console.log(typeof true);           // 'boolean'
console.log(typeof undefined);      // 'undefined'
console.log(typeof null);           // 'object' ← JS quirk!
console.log(typeof Symbol());       // 'symbol'
console.log(typeof {});             // 'object'
console.log(typeof []);             // 'object' (arrays are objects)
console.log(typeof function(){}); // 'function'
js.data_types.admin_notes: Common gotcha: typeof null === 'object'. Always mention this.
examples: what are the data types in javascript
what is the difference between null and undefined
what does typeof return
explain primitive vs reference types
why is typeof null object in javascript
what is a symbol in javascript
how many data types does javascript have

js.type_coercion.response:
Type coercion is JavaScript's automatic (or explicit) conversion of values from one type to another.

Implicit coercion (JS decides) — dangerous if unaware:
• + with a string converts the other operand to string
• ==  (loose equality) coerces before comparing
• Truthy/falsy rules govern if, &&, ||, !

Explicit coercion (you decide) — safe and clear:
• Number(), parseInt(), parseFloat() — to number
• String() or .toString() — to string
• Boolean() — to boolean

Always prefer === (strict equality) to avoid coercion surprises.
js.type_coercion.code_example:
// Implicit — surprising results
console.log(1 + '2');      // '12'  (number → string)
console.log('5' - 2);      // 3     (string → number)
console.log('' == false);  // true  (loose equality)
console.log(0 == false);   // true

// Explicit — predictable
console.log(Number('42')); // 42
console.log(String(99));   // '99'
console.log(Boolean(0));   // false
console.log(Boolean(''));  // false

// Always use strict equality
console.log(1 === '1');    // false
js.type_coercion.admin_notes: Source of many bugs. Stress === over ==.

why does 1 plus 2 equal 12 in javascript
difference between == and === javascript
what are truthy and falsy values
explain implicit vs explicit type conversion
why does empty string equal false
how to convert string to number in javascript

js.scope.response:
Scope determines where variables are accessible in your code.

• Global scope — declared outside any function/block; accessible everywhere.
• Function scope — variables declared with var inside a function; only visible inside that function.
• Block scope — variables declared with let/const inside {} (if, for, etc.); only visible inside that block.
• Module scope — each ES6 module file has its own scope by default.

The scope chain: when JS looks for a variable, it starts in the current scope and works outward to the global scope.
js.scope.admin_notes: Foundation concept — needed before closures and hoisting.

difference between local and global variables
what is lexical scope

js.hoisting.response:
Hoisting is JavaScript's behaviour of moving declarations to the top of their scope before code executes (conceptually — the engine does this during the compilation phase).

• Function declarations are fully hoisted — you can call them before they appear in the file.
• var declarations are hoisted but initialised to undefined — you can reference them before declaration but get undefined.
• let and const are hoisted but NOT initialised — accessing them before declaration throws a ReferenceError. This gap is called the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ).
• Function expressions (const fn = function(){}) follow the rules of their keyword (let/const), so they are NOT callable before the line of assignment.
js.hoisting.admin_notes: Common interview topic. Temporal Dead Zone is the key differentiator between var and let/const.
examples: what is hoisting in javascript
explain the temporal dead zone

what happens when you hoist a var variable
difference between hoisting of var and let

what is TDZ in javascript

js.functions.response:
A function is a reusable block of code. JavaScript has several ways to define one:

1. Function Declaration — fully hoisted; best for named, reusable logic.
2. Function Expression — assigned to a variable; not hoisted as callable.
3. Arrow Function — concise syntax; inherits this from surrounding context; no own arguments object.
4. Default Parameters — give parameters fallback values.
5. Rest Parameters — collect remaining arguments into an array.

Functions are first-class in JS: they can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
js.functions.admin_notes: Foundational. Link to arrow functions and closures.

how to write an arrow function
what are default parameters in javascript
what are rest parameters
explain first class functions
how to define a function in javascript

js.arrow_functions.response:
Arrow functions (=>) are a shorter syntax for writing functions, introduced in ES6. Key differences from regular functions:

1. Concise syntax — single-expression body can omit braces and return.
2. No own this — they inherit this from the surrounding lexical scope. This is the most important behavioural difference.
3. No arguments object — use rest parameters instead.
4. Cannot be used as constructors — no new keyword.
5. No prototype property.

Use arrow functions for callbacks and short helpers. Use regular functions when you need their own this (e.g., object methods, constructors).
js.arrow_functions.admin_notes: The this behaviour is the critical concept. Use timer/callback example to illustrate.

can an arrow function be a constructor
arrow function syntax in javascript

js.closures.response:
A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) lexical scope, even after that outer function has finished executing.

Every function in JavaScript forms a closure over the variables in its surrounding scope.

Why closures matter:
• Data privacy — variables in the outer scope can't be accessed from outside, only through the returned inner function.
• State persistence — the inner function remembers values between calls.
• Factory functions and module patterns.

Closures are not a special syntax — they are a consequence of how lexical scoping works.
js.closures.admin_notes: Intermediate concept. Include the loop var/let closure bug — examiners love it.

explain closures with an example

why does my inner function remember variables
what is lexical scope and closures
closure with a counter example

closure bug in for loop with var

js.this_keyword.response:
this refers to the object that is currently executing the function. Its value is determined by HOW the function is called, not where it is defined (except for arrow functions).

Binding rules (in priority order):
1. new binding — using new, this = the new object being created.
2. Explicit binding — using .call(obj), .apply(obj), or .bind(obj), this = obj.
3. Implicit binding — called as a method: obj.fn(), this = obj.
4. Default binding — standalone call: fn(), this = global (or undefined in strict mode).
5. Arrow functions — no own this; inherit from enclosing lexical scope.

Common mistake: extracting a method from an object loses its this binding.
js.this_keyword.admin_notes: Very common interview question. 4 binding rules + arrow function exception.

js.prototypes.response:
JavaScript is prototype-based. Every object has an internal link to another object called its prototype. When you access a property that doesn't exist on the object, JS walks up the prototype chain until it finds it or reaches null.

Key concepts:
• Object.prototype is the root of every prototype chain.
• __proto__ — the internal prototype link (avoid using directly).
• Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) — the standard way to get an object's prototype.
• prototype property — only exists on functions; used as the prototype for objects created with new.
• Object.create(proto) — creates a new object with proto as its prototype.

ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototype inheritance.
js.prototypes.admin_notes: Medium difficulty. Emphasise the chain lookup mechanism and that classes are sugar over this.

explain the prototype chain

difference between prototype and __proto__

what is hasOwnProperty

js.classes.response:
ES6 classes provide a cleaner syntax for creating objects and implementing inheritance. They are syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance — under the hood, the same prototype chain is used.

Key features:
• constructor() — runs when new is called.
• Methods defined in the class body are placed on the prototype.
• extends — sets up prototype chain inheritance.
• super() — must be called in a child constructor before using this.
• Static methods — called on the class itself, not instances.
• Private fields (#) — truly private, only accessible inside the class (ES2022).
js.classes.admin_notes: Classes are syntactic sugar — mention prototype connection. Private fields (#) are modern and important.

explain class inheritance in javascript

js.arrays.response:
Arrays store ordered lists of values. JavaScript arrays are dynamic, can hold mixed types, and have a rich set of built-in methods.

Must-know array methods:
• map(fn) — transforms every element, returns new array.
• filter(fn) — keeps elements where fn returns true.
• reduce(fn, init) — accumulates a single value.
• forEach(fn) — iterates, no return value.
• find(fn) — returns first matching element.
• findIndex(fn) — returns index of first match.
• some(fn) — true if at least one element matches.
• every(fn) — true if all elements match.
• flat(depth) — flattens nested arrays.
• flatMap(fn) — map then flatten one level.
• includes(val) — checks if value exists.
• splice / slice — mutate / copy portions.
• sort(fn) — sorts in place (mutates!).
js.arrays.admin_notes: map/filter/reduce are the most tested. Include chaining example.

js.objects.response:
Objects are collections of key-value pairs (properties). They are the foundation of JavaScript.

Key object operations:
• Object literal — simplest way to create: { key: value }
• Property access — dot notation (obj.key) or bracket notation (obj['key'])
• Computed property names — dynamic keys in literals
• Shorthand properties and methods (ES6)
• Object.keys() / Object.values() / Object.entries() — iterate over properties
• Object.assign() — shallow merge
• Spread operator {...obj} — shallow copy / merge
• Object.freeze() — prevent modifications
• Optional chaining (?.) — safe deep access
js.objects.admin_notes: Foundation of JS data modelling. Optional chaining is frequently missed by beginners.

js.destructuring.response:
Destructuring lets you extract values from arrays or objects into distinct variables using a matching pattern on the left side of the assignment.

Object destructuring:
• Extract named properties, with optional renaming and default values.

Array destructuring:
• Extract elements by position, with optional skipping and defaults.

Use cases:
• Cleaner function parameter handling.
• Swapping variables.
• Extracting from API responses.
• Importing specific exports.
js.destructuring.admin_notes: Very common in modern JS and React. Include renaming and defaults.

js.spread_rest.response:
The spread (...) and rest (...) operators share the same syntax but serve opposite purposes.

Spread — expands an iterable (array, object, string) into individual elements:
• Copy arrays/objects
• Merge arrays/objects
• Pass array elements as function arguments

Rest — collects remaining elements into an array:
• In function parameters: gather extra arguments
• In destructuring: gather remaining items

Note: both are shallow — nested objects/arrays are still references.
js.spread_rest.admin_notes: Spread vs rest is a classic confusion. Contrast by position in code.

js.loops.response:
JavaScript provides several loop constructs:

• for — classic; best when you need the index or a specific count.
• while — repeats while condition is true; good when iterations are unknown.
• do...while — executes body at least once, then checks condition.
• for...of — iterates over iterable values (arrays, strings, maps, sets). Use for arrays.
• for...in — iterates over object keys (enumerable properties). Use for objects, not arrays.
• Array methods (forEach, map, filter) — functional alternatives; often cleaner for collections.

breakexits a loop early. continue skips the rest of the current iteration.
js.loops.admin_notes: Common confusion: for...of vs for...in. Emphasise for...in is for objects, for...of is for iterables.

js.promises.response:
A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation. It can be in one of three states:
• pending — initial state, operation not done yet.
• fulfilled — operation completed successfully; .then() handlers run.
• rejected — operation failed; .catch() handlers run.

Key methods:
• .then(onFulfilled, onRejected) — chain success handlers.
• .catch(onRejected) — handle errors.
• .finally(fn) — runs regardless of outcome.
• Promise.all([...]) — wait for all to fulfil (fails fast on rejection).
• Promise.allSettled([...]) — wait for all to settle, regardless of outcome.
• Promise.race([...]) — resolves/rejects as soon as the first one does.
• Promise.any([...]) — fulfils as soon as one succeeds.
js.promises.admin_notes: Core async concept. Link to async/await. Promise.all vs allSettled is a common interview question.

how to chain promises

js.async_await.response:
async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises, making asynchronous code look and feel like synchronous code.

• async function — always returns a Promise. You can await inside it.
• await — pauses execution of the async function until the Promise resolves/rejects. The rest of the program is NOT blocked.
• Error handling — wrap await calls in try/catch.
• Parallel execution — avoid sequential awaits when tasks are independent; use Promise.all instead.

await can only be used inside an async function (or at the top level of a module in modern environments).
js.async_await.admin_notes: Most practical async topic. Stress the sequential vs parallel pattern — very common mistake.

how does async await work

how to run multiple awaits in parallel

error handling in async functions

js.event_loop.response:
JavaScript is single-threaded — it can only do one thing at a time. The event loop is the mechanism that allows JS to perform non-blocking operations despite this.

Components:
• Call Stack — where function calls live. Only one frame executes at a time.
• Web APIs / Node APIs — handle async work (timers, fetch, I/O) outside the call stack.
• Callback Queue (Macrotask Queue) — completed async callbacks wait here (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O).
• Microtask Queue — higher-priority queue for Promise .then() callbacks and queueMicrotask().
• Event Loop — continuously checks: if call stack is empty, push the next microtask (all of them first), then the next macrotask.

Microtasks always run BEFORE the next macrotask.
js.event_loop.admin_notes: Advanced but very popular interview question. The microtask vs macrotask order surprises most candidates.

js.dom.response:
The DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree-structured, in-memory representation of an HTML document that JavaScript can read and modify.

Selecting elements:
• document.getElementById('id') — single element by ID.
• document.querySelector('css') — first match for a CSS selector.
• document.querySelectorAll('css') — NodeList of all matches.

Modifying elements:
• element.textContent — set/get text (safe — no HTML parsing).
• element.innerHTML — set/get HTML markup (risk of XSS if user input).
• element.setAttribute / getAttribute / removeAttribute
• element.classList.add / remove / toggle / contains
• element.style.property = 'value'

Creating and inserting:
• document.createElement('tag')
• parent.appendChild(child) / parent.prepend(child)
• element.remove()
js.dom.admin_notes: Critical for web dev. Warn about innerHTML + user input = XSS.

how to change style with javascript

js.events.response:
Events let JavaScript react to user interactions and browser changes.

Handling events:
• addEventListener(type, handler) — preferred; supports multiple handlers and removal.
• onclick, onfocus, etc. — inline or property-based; only one handler per event.

The Event object is passed to every handler — use it to get target, prevent default behaviour, or stop propagation.

Event bubbling: events fire on the target then bubble up through ancestor elements.
Event delegation: attach ONE listener on a parent to handle events from many child elements — efficient and handles dynamically added elements.

Common events: click, input, change, submit, keydown, keyup, mouseover, resize, DOMContentLoaded, load.
js.events.admin_notes: Event delegation is an important pattern for performance. Bubbling must be explained first.

js.modules.response:
JavaScript modules let you split code into separate files and explicitly control what is shared.

ES Modules (ESM) — modern standard:
• export — mark values/functions/classes to be shared.
• import — bring them into another file.
• Named exports — export multiple things by name.
• Default export — one per file, imported with any name.
• import() — dynamic import, returns a Promise (lazy loading).

CommonJS (CJS) — Node.js legacy:
• module.exports / exports — export.
• require() — import.

Key difference: ESM is static (analysed at compile time), CJS is dynamic (evaluated at runtime). Modern Node.js and all browsers support ESM.
js.modules.admin_notes: ESM vs CJS is important for Node.js context. Dynamic import is relevant for performance.

js.error_handling.response:
Errors in JavaScript can be caught and handled gracefully using try/catch/finally.

Built-in Error types:
• Error — generic base.
• TypeError — wrong type (e.g., calling null as a function).
• ReferenceError — accessing an undeclared variable.
• SyntaxError — invalid JS syntax (thrown at parse time).
• RangeError — value out of allowed range.

Best practices:
• Catch only what you can handle; re-throw what you can't.
• Use finally for cleanup (close files, hide loading spinners).
• Create custom Error subclasses for domain-specific errors.
• In async code, combine try/catch with await.
• Don't swallow errors silently with an empty catch block.
js.error_handling.admin_notes: Custom errors and re-throwing are interview favourites.

js.callbacks.response:
A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be executed after some operation completes. It's the oldest pattern for handling asynchronous operations in JavaScript.

Synchronous callbacks run immediately (e.g., forEach, map).
Asynchronous callbacks run later (e.g., setTimeout, event listeners, old Node.js APIs).

Callback hell: deeply nested callbacks become hard to read and reason about. This is why Promises and async/await were introduced.

Callbacks are still valid and widely used for event listeners and simple one-off async operations.
js.callbacks.admin_notes: Teach callbacks before promises — they provide the conceptual foundation.

js.higher_order_functions.response:
A higher-order function (HOF) is a function that either:
1. Takes one or more functions as arguments, or
2. Returns a function.

This is possible because JavaScript treats functions as first-class citizens — they can be stored in variables, passed around, and returned like any other value.

Higher-order functions are the foundation of functional programming in JS. You use them every day: map, filter, reduce, addEventListener, setTimeout, and any function that accepts a callback.
js.higher_order_functions.admin_notes: Bridges callbacks and functional programming. Reinforce with map/filter/reduce examples.

js.currying.response:
Currying is a technique of transforming a function that takes multiple arguments into a sequence of functions that each take one argument.

f(a, b, c) → f(a)(b)(c)

Why it's useful:
• Partial application — pre-fill some arguments to create specialised functions.
• Function composition — chain small, single-argument functions.
• Reusability — create configurable utility functions.

Currying doesn't call the function; it transforms how it is called. Partial application calls the function with some arguments fixed.
js.currying.admin_notes: Advanced functional programming topic. Often confused with partial application — distinguish them.

js.generators.response:
A generator function uses the function* syntax and returns a Generator object. It can pause its own execution with yield and resume it on demand.

Key concepts:
• function* — declares a generator.
• yield — pauses the generator and returns a value.
• .next() — resumes execution until the next yield or return.
• The return value of .next() is { value, done }.
• Generators are iterables — usable in for...of loops.

Use cases:
• Lazy data sequences (infinite sequences without crashing memory).
• Custom iterators.
• Asynchronous flow control (older pattern, mostly replaced by async/await).
js.generators.admin_notes: Advanced topic. Focus on the pause/resume mental model and lazy sequences.

js.iterators.response:
An iterator is any object with a .next() method that returns { value, done }. An iterable is any object with a [Symbol.iterator]() method that returns an iterator.

Built-in iterables: arrays, strings, maps, sets, NodeLists, arguments, generators.

The for...of loop, spread operator, and destructuring all work on any iterable by calling [Symbol.iterator]().

You can make any object iterable by implementing [Symbol.iterator]. This is the protocol that powers generators and many ES6 features.
js.iterators.admin_notes: Often overlooked but explains how for...of works under the hood. Link to generators.

how to make a custom iterable object

what is the iteration protocol in javascript

js.map_set.response:
Map and Set are modern data structures (ES6+) that complement objects and arrays.

Map — key-value pairs where keys can be ANY type (including objects and functions):
• Better performance than objects for frequent additions/deletions.
• Maintains insertion order.
• Has a .size property.
• Methods: .set(k,v), .get(k), .has(k), .delete(k), .clear(), .forEach(), .entries(), .keys(), .values().

Set — collection of UNIQUE values:
• Automatically removes duplicates.
• Methods: .add(v), .has(v), .delete(v), .size, .forEach(), .keys().

WeakMap and WeakSet — hold weak references; keys/values can be garbage collected when no longer referenced elsewhere. Useful for private data and caches.
js.map_set.admin_notes: Map vs Object and Set for deduplication are very practical. WeakMap for private data is advanced.

what is a Set in javascript

when should I use a Map instead of an object
how to iterate over a Map

js.symbols.response:
Symbol is a primitive data type that creates a guaranteed unique identifier. Every call to Symbol() creates a value that is not equal to any other value — ever.

Use cases:
• Unique property keys — prevent accidental key collisions in objects.
• Well-known Symbols — built-in hooks: Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive, Symbol.hasInstance, etc.
• Private-ish properties — Symbols are not enumerable and not returned by Object.keys().

Note: Symbol.for('key') creates a global symbol — the same key always returns the same Symbol.
js.symbols.admin_notes: Advanced. Focus on uniqueness guarantee and Symbol as property key. Well-known symbols are key for understanding iterators.

js.proxy_reflect.response:
Proxy wraps an object and intercepts fundamental operations on it (property access, assignment, function calls, etc.) using handler traps.

Reflect is a built-in object that provides methods mirroring the Proxy traps. It allows you to perform the default operation inside a trap without re-implementing it manually.

Common Proxy traps:
• get(target, prop) — intercept property reads.
• set(target, prop, value) — intercept property writes.
• has(target, prop) — intercept the in operator.
• deleteProperty / apply / construct — and more.

Use cases: validation, logging, virtual properties, reactive data systems (Vue 3 uses Proxy for reactivity).
js.proxy_reflect.admin_notes: Advanced metaprogramming. Mention Vue 3 reactivity as a real-world example.

js.regex.response:
Regular expressions (regex) are patterns used to match, search, and manipulate text.

Creating a regex:
• Literal: /pattern/flags
• Constructor: new RegExp('pattern', 'flags')

Flags:
• g — global (find all matches)
• i — case-insensitive
• m — multiline

Essential methods:
• regex.test(str) — returns true/false
• str.match(regex) — returns matches
• str.replace(regex, replacement) — replace matches
• str.split(regex) — split by pattern

Common patterns:
• \d — digit, \w — word character, \s — whitespace
• ^ — start, $ — end, . — any character
• + — one or more, * — zero or more, ? — zero or one
• {n,m} — between n and m occurrences
• [] — character class, () — group, | — or
js.regex.admin_notes: Practical but complex. Focus on test(), match(), replace(). Named groups are ES2018 and impressive.

how to use regex in javascript

how to use match and replace with regex

how to extract numbers from a string with regex

js.json.response:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text format for data exchange. It's a string, not a JS object — but it looks very similar.

Key operations:
• JSON.stringify(obj, replacer, space) — converts a JS value to a JSON string.
• JSON.parse(str, reviver) — converts a JSON string back to a JS value.

JSON rules (stricter than JS):
• Keys must be double-quoted strings.
• No comments, no trailing commas, no undefined, no functions.
• Values: string, number, boolean, null, array, object.

Deep clone trick: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) — quick but loses functions, dates, undefined, and circular references.
js.json.admin_notes: Very practical for API work. Deep clone caveat is important — structuredClone() is the modern alternative.

js.template_literals.response:
Template literals (backtick strings) are an ES6 way to create strings that support:
• Embedded expressions — ${expression} is evaluated and inserted.
• Multi-line strings — no need for \n or string concatenation.
• Tagged templates — a function before the backtick can process the template.

Template literals replace most cases where you'd use string concatenation with +.
js.template_literals.admin_notes: Beginner-friendly. Most impactful upgrade from old JS. Tagged templates are advanced.

how to write multi-line strings in javascript

js.optional_chaining.response:
Optional chaining (?.) safely accesses nested object properties without throwing a TypeError if an intermediate value is null or undefined — it just returns undefined instead.

Variants:
• obj?.prop — property access.
• obj?.[expr] — computed property access.
• func?.() — function call (only calls if func is not null/undefined).

Couple with nullish coalescing (??) to provide a default value.
js.optional_chaining.admin_notes: Extremely common in modern JS. Pair with ?? for default values.
examples: what is optional chaining in javascript
how does ?. work in javascript
how to safely access nested object properties
what is nullish coalescing
difference between optional chaining and logical and

optional chaining with function calls

js.nullish_coalescing.response:
The nullish coalescing operator (??) returns the right-hand side operand only when the left-hand side is null or undefined.

This is different from the logical OR (||) operator, which returns the right side for any falsy value (including 0, '', false, NaN).

Use ?? when 0, empty string, or false are valid values that you don't want to replace with a default.
js.nullish_coalescing.admin_notes: Often confused with ||. The key insight is that ?? only triggers for null/undefined, not all falsy values.
examples: what is nullish coalescing in javascript
what does ?? do in javascript
difference between ?? and || in javascript
when to use ?? vs ||

nullish coalescing with optional chaining

js.object_immutability.response:
JavaScript offers several levels of object immutability:

• const — only prevents reassignment of the variable binding; the object itself is still mutable.
• Object.freeze(obj) — shallow freeze: prevents adding, removing, or changing properties. Nested objects remain mutable.
• Object.seal(obj) — allows changing existing properties but prevents adding or removing.
• structuredClone(obj) — creates a true deep clone (ES2022, replaces JSON trick).

For true deep immutability, use recursive freezing or a library like Immer.
js.object_immutability.admin_notes: Common misconception: const !== immutable. Clarify shallow vs deep freeze.
examples: how to make an object immutable in javascript

what is Object.seal
difference between freeze and seal

js.date.response:
The Date object handles dates and times in JavaScript.

Creating dates:
• new Date() — current date/time.
• new Date('2025-06-15') — from ISO string.
• new Date(year, month, day, ...) — month is 0-indexed (0 = January).
• Date.now() — current timestamp in milliseconds.

Common methods:
• .getFullYear(), .getMonth() (0-indexed!), .getDate(), .getDay()
• .getHours(), .getMinutes(), .getSeconds()
• .toISOString(), .toLocaleDateString(), .toLocaleTimeString()

Date arithmetic: subtract two Date objects to get milliseconds difference.

Note: For complex date operations, use libraries like dayjs or date-fns.
js.date.admin_notes: Month 0-index is an infamous gotcha. Always mention it.

js.math.response:
The Math object provides mathematical constants and functions. It is not a constructor — all properties and methods are static.

Useful constants:
• Math.PI — π ≈ 3.14159
• Math.E — Euler's number ≈ 2.71828

Useful methods:
• Math.round(n) — rounds to nearest integer.
• Math.floor(n) — rounds down.
• Math.ceil(n) — rounds up.
• Math.trunc(n) — removes decimal (towards 0).
• Math.abs(n) — absolute value.
• Math.max(...args) / Math.min(...args) — largest/smallest.
• Math.sqrt(n), Math.pow(base, exp) — square root, power.
• Math.random() — random float [0, 1).
• Math.log(n), Math.log2(n), Math.log10(n) — logarithms.
js.math.admin_notes: Very practical. Random integer recipe is a classic snippet everyone needs.

js.string_methods.response:
Strings in JavaScript are immutable — every method returns a new string.

Essential string methods:
• .length — number of characters.
• .toUpperCase() / .toLowerCase() — case conversion.
• .trim() / .trimStart() / .trimEnd() — remove whitespace.
• .includes(str) — boolean substring check.
• .startsWith(str) / .endsWith(str).
• .indexOf(str) / .lastIndexOf(str) — position of substring (-1 if not found).
• .slice(start, end) — extract substring.
• .split(separator) — split into array.
• .replace(search, replacement) / .replaceAll().
• .padStart(len, char) / .padEnd(len, char).
• .repeat(n).
• .charAt(i) / [i] — character at index.
• Template literals for concatenation.
js.string_methods.admin_notes: Extremely practical. split/join, slice, and replace are the most used.

js.fetch_api.response:
The Fetch API is the modern, Promise-based way to make HTTP requests from the browser (and Node.js 18+).

How fetch works:
1. fetch(url, options) returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object.
2. Check response.ok (true for 2xx status codes).
3. Parse the body: response.json(), response.text(), response.blob().
4. The Response body can only be consumed ONCE.

fetch does NOT reject on HTTP errors (404, 500) — it only rejects on network failures. You must check response.ok manually.

For POST requests, pass method, headers, and body in the options object.
js.fetch_api.admin_notes: Critical for web dev. Emphasise: fetch doesn't throw on 404/500 — must check response.ok.

js.local_storage.response:
The Web Storage API provides localStorage and sessionStorage for storing key-value pairs in the browser.

localStorage:
• Persists indefinitely (until manually cleared or user clears browser data).
• Shared across all tabs of the same origin.
• Storage limit: ~5–10 MB.

sessionStorage:
• Cleared when the tab/window is closed.
• Not shared between tabs.

Both only store strings — use JSON.stringify/parse for objects.

Methods: .setItem(key, val), .getItem(key), .removeItem(key), .clear(), .key(i), .length.
js.local_storage.admin_notes: Very practical. Warn about storing sensitive data and the string-only limitation.
